abstract: Anticipating diverse future states is a central challenge in video world modeling. Discriminative world models produce a deterministic prediction that implicitly averages over possible futures, while existing generative world models remain computationally expensive. Recent work demonstrates that predicting the future in the feature space of a vision foundation model (VFM), rather than a latent space optimized for pixel reconstruction, requires significantly fewer world model parameters. However, most such approaches remain discriminative. In this work, we introduce DeltaTok, a tokenizer that encodes the VFM feature difference between consecutive frames into a single continuous "delta" token, and DeltaWorld, a generative world model operating on these tokens to efficiently generate diverse plausible futures. Delta tokens reduce video from a three-dimensional spatio-temporal representation to a one-dimensional temporal sequence, for example yielding a 1,024x token reduction with 512x512 frames. This compact representation enables tractable multi-hypothesis training, where many futures are generated in parallel and only the best is supervised. At inference, this leads to diverse predictions in a single forward pass. Experiments on dense forecasting tasks demonstrate that DeltaWorld forecasts futures that more closely align with real-world outcomes, while having over 35x fewer parameters and using 2,000x fewer FLOPs than existing generative world models. Code and weights: this https URL.

citations.fullCitation: [3] Federico Baldassarre, Marc Szafraniec, Basile Terver, Vasil Khalidov, Francisco Massa, Yann LeCun, Patrick Labatut, Maximilian Seitzer, and Piotr Bojanowski. Back to the Features: DINO as a Foundation for Video World Models. In ICML, 2025.
citations.justification: This citation is crucial as the presented work, DeltaWorld, directly builds upon the DINO-world architecture proposed in this paper. It serves as the primary discriminative baseline and established the paradigm of world modeling in the feature space of a vision foundation model.
citations.title: Back to the Features: DINO as a Foundation for Video World Models

citations.fullCitation: [1] Niket Agarwal, Arslan Ali, Maciej Bala, Yogesh Balaji, Erik Barker, Tiffany Cai, Prithvijit Chattopadhyay, Yongxin Chen, Yin Cui, Yifan Ding, et al. Cosmos World Foundation Model Platform for Physical AI. arXiv preprint arXiv:2501.03575, 2025.
citations.justification: This paper introduces Cosmos, the main state-of-the-art generative world model against which DeltaWorld's performance and computational efficiency are benchmarked. The authors highlight that DeltaWorld uses over 2,000x fewer FLOPs, making this comparison central to their claims of efficiency.
citations.title: Cosmos World Foundation Model Platform for Physical AI

citations.fullCitation: [5] Apratim Bhattacharyya, Bernt Schiele, and Mario Fritz. Accurate and Diverse Sampling of Sequences Based on a “Best of Many” Sample Objective. In CVPR, 2018.
citations.justification: This work is methodologically fundamental as it introduces the "Best-of-Many" (BoM) training objective. The authors of the main paper adopt this objective to transform their model into an efficient generative one that can produce diverse futures in a single forward pass.
citations.title: Accurate and Diverse Sampling of Sequences Based on a “Best of Many” Sample Objective

citations.fullCitation: [27] David Ha and Jürgen Schmidhuber. Recurrent World Models Facilitate Policy Evolution. NeurIPS, 2018.
citations.justification: This is a seminal paper that introduced and popularized the concept of "world models," which is the central research problem addressed by DeltaWorld. It provides the foundational motivation for learning predictive models of an environment to enable planning and reasoning.
citations.title: Recurrent World Models Facilitate Policy Evolution

intermediateReport:
The following report provides a detailed analysis of the research paper "A Frame is Worth One Token: Efficient Generative World Modeling with Delta Tokens."

### 1. Authors and Institution(s)

The research was conducted by:
*   Tommie Kerssies (Amazon, Eindhoven University of Technology)
*   Gabriele Berton (Amazon)
*   Ju He (Amazon)
*   Qihang Yu (Amazon)
*   Wufei Ma (Amazon, Johns Hopkins University)
*   Daan de Geus (Eindhoven University of Technology)
*   Gijs Dubbelman (Eindhoven University of Technology)
*   Liang-Chieh Chen (Amazon)

The work was performed while some authors were affiliated with Amazon. Daan de Geus and Gijs Dubbelman contributed with equal advising.

### 2. How This Work Fits into the Broader Research Landscape

Anticipating future states of the world, often referred to as world modeling, is a fundamental capability for autonomous systems like robots and vehicles. The future is inherently uncertain, necessitating predictions that account for multiple plausible outcomes rather than a single deterministic forecast.

Existing world models broadly fall into two categories:
*   **Discriminative World Models:** These models produce a single, deterministic prediction. While often computationally efficient, they tend to average over possible futures, resulting in predictions that may not correspond to any realistic outcome when uncertainty is high. This limitation restricts their utility in applications requiring robust decision-making across diverse scenarios, such as autonomous driving where anticipating multiple agent interactions is crucial for collision avoidance. Recent advancements in discriminative world models have shifted towards operating in the feature space of Vision Foundation Models (VFMs), rather than pixel space, demonstrating improved accuracy and reduced parameter counts for dense forecasting tasks. However, most of these remain discriminative.
*   **Generative World Models:** These models are designed to generate a set of diverse and plausible future states. While conceptually more suitable for uncertain environments, most existing generative world models face significant computational challenges. These inefficiencies stem from several factors:
    1.  **Pixel-level fidelity:** Many models aim for high-fidelity pixel reconstruction, which is computationally expensive and often unnecessary for downstream semantic tasks.
    2.  **Multiple sequential forward passes:** Approaches like diffusion models or autoregressive discrete token predictors typically require iterative or sequential computations to generate a single future hypothesis, making inference slow.
    3.  **Spatio-temporal redundancy:** Traditional video tokenizers encode each frame into a dense grid of spatial tokens. However, consecutive video frames often exhibit high redundancy, with only small portions of the scene changing, leading to long sequences of redundant tokens and inefficient processing.

This research aims to address the computational inefficiencies of generative world models while preserving their ability to generate diverse futures. It builds upon the concept of operating in VFM feature space, a trend seen in discriminative models, and introduces novel techniques to exploit temporal redundancy and enable single-pass generation of multiple hypotheses. By doing so, this work positions itself as a contribution towards making generative world modeling more efficient and practical for real-world applications.

### 3. Key Objectives and Motivation

The primary objective of this work is to develop a generative world model that can efficiently generate diverse and plausible future states. This objective is driven by the identified limitations of existing approaches:

**Motivation and Specific Challenges Addressed:**

1.  **Addressing Deterministic Predictions in Uncertain Futures:**
    *   **Problem:** Discriminative world models produce a single, averaged prediction. In scenarios with inherent uncertainty (e.g., multiple possible trajectories for other vehicles), this deterministic output fails to represent the full spectrum of plausible outcomes. This "collapse to the conditional mean" can lead to suboptimal or unsafe decisions in real-world applications like autonomous driving.
    *   **Objective:** To enable the model to generate a *set* of plausible future states, moving beyond deterministic predictions to capture the inherent diversity of real-world events.
    *   **Proposed Solution:** Implement a generative mechanism, specifically the "Best-of-Many" (BoM) training objective, to map different stochastic inputs to distinct future hypotheses.

2.  **Improving Computational Efficiency of Generative Models:**
    *   **Problem (i): Pixel-level fidelity vs. semantic understanding:** Many generative models prioritize pixel-level reconstruction, which demands significant computational resources to model fine visual details. For downstream tasks like segmentation or depth estimation, these pixel-level details are often irrelevant; semantic features are more pertinent.
    *   **Objective:** To reduce computational load by focusing prediction on semantically relevant information rather than superfluous pixel details.
    *   **Proposed Solution:** Operate directly in the feature space of Vision Foundation Models (VFMs), which are pre-trained to provide rich semantic representations, thereby abstracting away pixel-level variability.

    *   **Problem (ii): Multiple sequential forward passes:** Conventional generative models (e.g., diffusion models, autoregressive discrete token models) typically require multiple iterative steps or sequential forward passes to generate a single future hypothesis. This process is computationally intensive and slow, especially for real-time applications.
    *   **Objective:** To enable the generation of multiple future hypotheses in a single forward pass, significantly speeding up inference.
    *   **Proposed Solution:** Combine the VFM feature space operation with the BoM training objective, allowing parallel generation of multiple futures from distinct noise queries.

    *   **Problem (iii): Spatio-temporal redundancy in video representation:** Natural video exhibits high spatio-temporal redundancy, where successive frames often differ only in structured and low-dimensional ways (e.g., moving objects, subtle scene changes). Standard tokenizers that represent each frame as a dense spatial feature map create long, redundant token sequences, burdening the model with predicting equally redundant outputs.
    *   **Objective:** To create a more compact and efficient representation of video dynamics by minimizing redundancy.
    *   **Proposed Solution:** Introduce "DeltaTok," a novel tokenizer designed to encode only the *difference* or change between consecutive VFM feature maps into a single, continuous "delta" token. This effectively collapses the 3D spatio-temporal video representation into a 1D temporal sequence.

By addressing these motivations through DeltaTok and the Best-of-Many training objective within a VFM feature space framework, the authors aim to establish DeltaWorld as an efficient and capable generative world model that generates diverse, plausible futures at a significantly reduced computational cost.

### 4. Methodology and Approach

The methodology outlined in the paper proceeds by progressively transforming a discriminative world model into an efficient generative one, integrating two main innovations: Best-of-Many (BoM) training and DeltaTok compression.

**4.1. Preliminaries: Building on a Discriminative World Model**
The work is founded on a discriminative model referred to as DINO-world, which directly models scene dynamics in the feature space of a frozen Vision Foundation Model (VFM), specifically DINOv3 ViT-B.
*   **Architecture:** Given a sequence of `t` video frames (`V_{1:t}`), a frozen VFM (`ϕ`) embeds each frame `v_i` into a grid of patch tokens `x_i ∈ R^(H×W×D)`. A future predictor `f`, composed of Transformer blocks, forecasts future patch tokens `x_{t+1,h,w}` conditioned on the context `X_{1:t}` and associated timestamps. This is performed independently for each spatial location `(h, w)` using a learnable query `q` and positional embeddings.
*   **Training & Inference:** The model is trained using a smooth L1 loss between predicted and ground-truth features, employing teacher forcing. During inference, it can perform autoregressive rollouts by appending predicted features to the context.

**4.2. Best-of-Many (BoM) Training for Generative Capabilities**
To extend the discriminative DINO-world into a generative model capable of diverse predictions, the authors adopt the Best-of-Many (BoM) training objective:
*   **Problem:** The regression loss in discriminative models drives predictions towards a single conditional mean, failing to capture diverse plausible outcomes.
*   **Approach:** During training, instead of a single learned query `q`, `K` noise queries `q_k` are sampled from a Gaussian distribution. Each `q_k` generates a distinct prediction `x^k_{t+1,h,w}` for each spatial location.
*   **Supervision:** Only the prediction `k*` that is closest to the ground truth (measured by L1 loss across all spatial locations) is used for backpropagation. This encourages the model to map different noise inputs to different plausible futures without requiring multiple sequential forward passes.

**4.3. Frame Compression to a Single Token (Intermediate Step)**
To improve the efficiency of BoM training and prediction, especially when generating multiple spatial feature maps, an intermediate step of compressing each frame's feature map into a single token is explored:
*   **Tokenizer Architecture:** A continuous autoencoder is introduced. An encoder `g` compresses a VFM feature map `x_t` into a single frame token `z_t ∈ R^D`. A decoder `h` reconstructs the feature map `x_t` from `z_t`. Both are implemented as Transformer blocks.
*   **Tokenizer Training:** The tokenizer is trained separately using an L2 reconstruction loss (`L_tok = ||x_t - x̂_t||^2`) to ensure `z_t` captures essential information.
*   **Limitations:** While reducing computation, a single token has limited capacity to faithfully represent an entire frame's spatial content and subtle inter-frame variations, potentially limiting prediction accuracy.

**4.4. Delta Compression to a Single Token (DeltaTok)**
To overcome the limitations of full frame compression and efficiently represent temporal changes, the core innovation, DeltaTok, is introduced:
*   **Insight:** Consecutive frames differ in low-dimensional ways. Instead of re-encoding the entire scene, it is more efficient to encode only the *change* between frames, similar to interframe compression in video coding.
*   **DeltaTok Architecture:** The tokenizer architecture is adapted.
    *   **Encoder:** The DeltaTok encoder `g` now takes both the previous frame's feature map `x_{t-1}` and the current frame's feature map `x_t` as input to produce a single continuous "delta" token `z_t` that encodes the transformation from `x_{t-1}` to `x_t`.
    *   **Decoder:** The DeltaTok decoder `h` reconstructs the current frame features `x̂_t` by transforming the previous frame features `x_{t-1}` using the delta token `z_t`.
*   **DeltaTok Training:** Trained with the same L2 reconstruction loss, using pairs of `(x_{t-1}, x_t)` from sampled video frames. This allows `z_t` to encode varying degrees of change, from static scenes to large transitions.
*   **Benefits:** This approach collapses video from a 3D spatio-temporal representation to a 1D temporal sequence of delta tokens. It naturally handles occlusions and new objects by encoding transformations.

**4.5. DeltaWorld Model**
DeltaWorld combines the separately trained, frozen DeltaTok with the BoM-trained future predictor:
*   **Prediction:** The predictor `f` now operates on a sequence of past delta tokens (`Z_{1:t}`) to predict the next delta token `z_{t+1}`.
*   **Spatial Feature Recovery:** To obtain the corresponding spatial feature map, the predicted delta token `z_{t+1}` is passed to the DeltaTok decoder along with the previous frame's spatial features: `x̂_{t+1} = h(x_t, ẑ_{t+1})`.
*   **Training:** During training, the BoM objective selects the best candidate delta token directly in the compact delta token space, without needing to decode full spatial feature maps.
*   **Inference:** Different noise queries (`q_k`) yield diverse future delta tokens in a single forward pass, which can then be decoded into spatial features. For autoregressive rollouts, predicted delta tokens are iteratively appended to the context.

This methodology aims to achieve high efficiency by compressing temporal changes into single tokens, enabling a lightweight predictor and efficient parallel generation of diverse futures through BoM training, all within the semantic richness of VFM feature space.

### 5. Main Findings and Results

The research presents several key findings regarding the efficiency and performance of DeltaWorld in generative world modeling.

**5.1. Efficiency Gains**
*   **Token Reduction:** DeltaTok reduces video representation from a 3D spatio-temporal grid to a 1D temporal sequence. For example, a 512x512 frame, which would typically be represented by 1024 spatial tokens (with 16x16 patches), is compressed into a single "delta" token. This represents a 1,024× token reduction per frame.
*   **Computational Cost Reduction:** DeltaWorld demonstrates significantly lower computational requirements compared to existing generative world models.
    *   It has over **35× fewer parameters** than Cosmos-4B and Cosmos-12B (0.3B vs. 4B/12B, respectively).
    *   It uses over **2,000× fewer FLOPs** than Cosmos-4B and Cosmos-12B (3.1×10^4 GFLOPs vs. 6.0×10^7 / 6.4×10^7 GFLOPs for generating 20 samples).
    *   The predictor's FLOPs in DeltaWorld (operating on single delta tokens) become negligible compared to the DeltaTok decoder and VFM backbone, accounting for only 0.5% of total inference FLOPs for 20 samples with a short context.

**5.2. Performance on Dense Forecasting Benchmark**
DeltaWorld was evaluated on the dense forecasting benchmark, covering semantic segmentation (VSPW, Cityscapes) and monocular depth estimation (KITTI) for short (∼0.2s) and mid (∼0.6s) prediction horizons.
*   **Comparison to Generative Baselines (Cosmos-4B/12B):**
    *   DeltaWorld's "best-of-20" predictions consistently **surpass Cosmos** across all evaluated metrics (segmentation mIoU, depth RMSE). For instance, on Cityscapes mid-horizon mIoU, DeltaWorld achieved 55.4 compared to Cosmos-12B's 53.3.
    *   DeltaWorld also achieved **stronger mean scores** across nearly all metrics compared to Cosmos, indicating that its sampled diversity is realistic and not noisy. The gap between DeltaWorld’s best and mean scores was consistently larger than Cosmos’, suggesting more meaningful and distinct sample diversity.
*   **Comparison to Discriminative Baseline (DINO-world):**
    *   DeltaWorld’s "best-of-20" predictions **substantially outperform** the single deterministic prediction of the discriminative DINO-world (e.g., +5.6 mIoU on Cityscapes mid, +2.2 mIoU on VSPW mid). This highlights the benefit of diverse predictions in capturing actual future modes.
    *   DeltaWorld’s "mean-of-20" scores are **competitive with or modestly better/worse than** DINO-world (e.g., 51.3 vs. 49.8 mIoU on Cityscapes mid, 46.7 vs. 47.9 mIoU on VSPW mid). The mean performance recovering to the level of a discriminative model (optimized for mean prediction) is attributed to the natural prior of the delta formulation, where predicting "no change" effectively preserves the previous frame.

**5.3. Ablation Study: Steps Towards an Efficient Generative World Model**
An ablation study demonstrated the contribution of each methodological component:
*   **Discriminative Baseline:** DINO-world, performing 44.8 mIoU (VSPW mid) and 45.4 mIoU (Cityscapes mid).
*   **Best-of-Many (BoM) Training:** Applying BoM improved the "best" prediction (e.g., Cityscapes mid to 46.8) but sharply decreased the "mean" (e.g., to 31.1), and increased training time (∼5×). This indicated that raw BoM could produce degenerate predictions.
*   **Frame Compression:** Compressing each frame to a single token significantly reduced training time (0.4×) and memory (0.2×), and improved the "mean" over raw BoM. However, accuracy remained below the discriminative baseline due to limited representational capacity of a single token for a full frame.
*   **Delta Compression (DeltaWorld):** Encoding *temporal differences* into a single delta token substantially improved both "best" and "mean" metrics over frame compression, with mean scores recovering to discriminative baseline levels. This confirmed the benefit of focusing on change. DeltaWorld maintained efficiency, with predictor accounting for only 0.5% of FLOPs.

**5.4. Best-of-Many Sample Scaling**
Increasing the number of queries (K) during BoM training consistently improved the "best" score at inference, without saturation, suggesting the model continued to learn more accurate specific futures. The "mean" score modestly decreased but stabilized beyond K=64, indicating that increased diversity did not significantly compromise average prediction quality.

**5.5. Delta Tokens in Discriminative Models (Appendix C)**
The study also found that delta tokens could effectively improve efficiency in discriminative world models. When integrated into the DINO-world baseline, it maintained performance while reducing training time (0.5×) and memory (0.2×). Similarly, applying delta compression to DINO-Foresight (a different discriminative architecture) matched its performance while reducing token count by 2048×.

In summary, the main findings demonstrate that DeltaWorld achieves significant computational efficiency (orders of magnitude fewer parameters and FLOPs) while delivering superior generative performance in terms of diversity and alignment with real future outcomes compared to prior generative models, and competitive average performance with discriminative models, particularly in its best predictions.

### 6. Significance and Potential Impact

This research presents a notable advancement in the field of video world modeling, particularly in addressing the long-standing trade-off between generating diverse, plausible futures and maintaining computational efficiency.

**6.1. Advancement in Generative World Modeling:**
The introduction of DeltaTok and its integration into DeltaWorld provides a paradigm shift in how video dynamics can be represented and predicted. By compressing temporal differences into single tokens, the model moves beyond dense spatial representations, focusing computation on relevant changes rather than redundant information. This directly addresses the inefficiency issues of prior generative models that relied on pixel-level fidelity or cumbersome spatial tokenization. The Best-of-Many training objective enables efficient, single-pass generation of multiple diverse futures, circumventing the slow iterative processes common in diffusion or autoregressive models.

**6.2. Practical Applications and Accessibility:**
The substantial reduction in parameters (over 35×) and computational cost (over 2,000× fewer FLOPs) makes advanced generative world modeling significantly more accessible and practical for real-world applications. Industries such as autonomous driving, robotics, and advanced simulation can benefit from models that efficiently anticipate diverse future states. This efficiency allows for real-time decision-making where exploring multiple hypotheses is critical for safety and optimal planning, without incurring prohibitive computational overheads.

**6.3. Novel Representation for Video Understanding:**
Delta tokens propose a compact, purely temporal representation for video, effectively collapsing a three-dimensional spatio-temporal signal into a one-dimensional sequence. This representation demonstrates efficacy not only in generative world models but also in enhancing the efficiency of discriminative models (as shown in the appendices). This suggests a broader potential for delta tokens to simplify and accelerate various video understanding and generation tasks, potentially influencing future architectural designs for video processing pipelines.

**6.4. Foundation for Future Research and Scalability:**
DeltaWorld lays foundational groundwork for scaling world models. Its efficiency facilitates the exploration of larger predictor sizes, longer context lengths, and deeper autoregressive rollouts, which were previously constrained by computational limitations. Future work can build upon this compact representation to develop more sophisticated models for complex scenarios. Additionally, the paper identifies areas for improvement, such as enhancing distributional modeling within the BoM framework and mitigating error accumulation during autoregressive delta token decoding, thus opening new avenues for research.

In summary, DeltaWorld represents a significant step towards practical and scalable generative world models. By demonstrating that efficient, diverse future forecasting is achievable with a compact, temporal-focused representation, this work has the potential to impact the development of intelligent agents that can reason effectively about the uncertain dynamics of complex environments.
overview:
## The Challenge of Predicting an Uncertain World

Predicting the future state of an environment, often termed "world modeling," is a core requirement for autonomous systems like self-driving cars or robotic manipulators. To navigate safely, these agents must not only understand the current scene but also anticipate how it will evolve over time. However, the real world is inherently non-deterministic. For instance, when a car approaches an intersection, a pedestrian might cross the street or wait on the sidewalk. Both outcomes are plausible.

Traditional world models often struggle with this uncertainty. Many existing approaches are "discriminative," meaning they produce a single, deterministic forecast. When faced with multiple possible futures, these models often output an average of all likely outcomes. In practice, this results in "blurry" or unrealistic predictions that do not represent any single plausible reality—a phenomenon known as collapsing to the conditional mean.

![Anticipating future states is a fundamental capability for autonomous systems.](https://paper-assets.alphaxiv.org/figures-normalized/figures/2604.04913v1/frame_1.png)

Generative world models aim to solve this by producing a diverse set of possible futures. While they are conceptually better suited for uncertain environments, they face significant computational hurdles. Many of these models attempt to reconstruct futures at the pixel level, which requires immense processing power for fine visual details that may be irrelevant to the agent's actual task (like recognizing a car vs. seeing the texture of its paint). Furthermore, most generative models require sequential forward passes—generating one frame at a time or iterating through many steps—making them too slow for real-time application.

"A Frame is Worth One Token: Efficient Generative World Modeling with Delta Tokens" introduces DeltaWorld, a framework designed to bridge the gap between high-fidelity generative modeling and computational efficiency. By operating in the semantic feature space of Vision Foundation Models (VFMs) and using a novel "delta token" compression technique, the authors achieve significant speedups and parameter reductions while maintaining the ability to generate diverse, high-quality future states.

## Beyond Pixels: Modeling in Semantic Feature Space

A key insight of DeltaWorld is that for many downstream tasks, such as semantic segmentation or depth estimation, pixel-level accuracy is secondary to semantic understanding. To leverage this, the model operates entirely within the feature space of a frozen Vision Foundation Model (VFM). Specifically, the authors utilize DINOv3 (ViT-B), which has been pre-trained on massive datasets to provide rich, semantically meaningful representations of images.

In this setup, each video frame $v_i$ in a sequence $V_{1:t}$ is passed through the frozen VFM $\phi$. This produces a grid of patch tokens $x_i \in \mathbb{R}^{H \times W \times D}$. Instead of trying to predict the billions of color values in a raw video, the world model focuses on predicting the evolution of these high-dimensional semantic features. This abstraction allows the model to ignore superfluous details like lighting changes or complex textures, focusing instead on the movement and transformation of objects and scene structures.

## DeltaTok: Exploiting Temporal Redundancy

In typical video sequences, consecutive frames are highly redundant. Most of the background remains stationary, and only a small fraction of the pixels change significantly from one frame to the next. Standard video tokenizers often ignore this, encoding each frame into a full spatial grid of tokens. If a frame is represented by a $32 \times 32$ grid, the model must process 1,024 tokens for every single timestep.

To address this, the authors introduce "DeltaTok," a novel tokenizer designed to compress the *change* between frames into a single continuous token. Unlike a standard autoencoder that might compress an entire frame into one token (which often loses too much spatial detail), DeltaTok uses the previous frame as a reference point.

The DeltaTok architecture consists of an encoder $g$ and a decoder $h$. The encoder takes the feature maps of the previous frame $x_{t-1}$ and the current frame $x_t$ and produces a single "delta" token $z_t \in \mathbb{R}^D$:

$$z_t = g(x_{t-1}, x_t)$$

The decoder then reconstructs the current frame features $\hat{x}_t$ by applying this delta token to the features of the previous frame:

$$\hat{x}_t = h(x_{t-1}, z_t)$$

The tokenizer is trained with a simple reconstruction loss:

$$L_{tok} = \|x_t - \hat{x}_t\|^2$$

By focusing only on the transformation between frames, DeltaTok can represent complex scene dynamics with a $1,024 \times$ reduction in token count per frame compared to standard grid-based tokenization. This effectively collapses a 3D spatio-temporal problem (height, width, time) into a 1D temporal sequence of delta tokens.

## Learning Diversity with Best-of-Many Training

Once the video is represented as a compact sequence of delta tokens $Z_{1:t}$, a future predictor $f$ is trained to forecast the next delta token $z_{t+1}$. To make this model generative and capable of producing diverse futures, the authors utilize a "Best-of-Many" (BoM) training objective.

In standard regression training, the model is penalized for any deviation from the single ground-truth future. In BoM training, the model is given $K$ different "noise queries" $q_k$ (sampled from a Gaussian distribution) alongside the context. Each query generates a different hypothesis for the future delta token. During training, the loss is only backpropagated through the "best" prediction—the one closest to the actual observed ground truth:

$$k^* = \arg\min_{k} \sum_{h,w} \|x_{t+1,h,w} - \hat{x}^k_{t+1,h,w}\|$$

This approach encourages the model to map different noise inputs to different plausible future outcomes. Because the model is not forced to average its predictions, it can learn to represent distinct modes of behavior (e.g., the car turns left in sample 1, but goes straight in sample 2). Crucially, because all $K$ hypotheses are generated in a single forward pass through the transformer, this process is much faster than the iterative sampling required by diffusion models.

![DeltaWorld generates multiple diverse hypotheses for the future in a single pass.](https://paper-assets.alphaxiv.org/figures-normalized/figures/2604.04913v1/x1.png)
*Caption: Example of diverse semantic segmentation futures generated by DeltaWorld. Each sample represents a distinct, plausible evolution of the scene.*

## System Architecture and Inference

The full DeltaWorld pipeline operates as follows:
1.  **Encoding:** A sequence of past frames is converted into VFM features.
2.  **Compression:** These features are passed through the DeltaTok encoder to create a sequence of historical delta tokens $Z_{1:t}$.
3.  **Prediction:** The future predictor $f$, conditioned on the past delta tokens and a set of noise queries, predicts $K$ possible future delta tokens $z_{t+1}$.
4.  **Decoding:** Each predicted delta token is combined with the current frame features $x_t$ via the DeltaTok decoder to recover the full spatial feature grid $\hat{x}_{t+1} = h(x_t, \hat{z}_{t+1})$.
5.  **Task Heads:** These predicted features can then be passed to lightweight decoders for specific tasks like depth estimation or semantic segmentation.

For longer forecasts, the model can operate autoregressively by appending its own predicted delta tokens back into the context and repeating the process.

## Empirical Results: Efficiency and Accuracy

The authors compared DeltaWorld against several benchmarks, including the large-scale Cosmos world models developed by NVIDIA. The results highlight a massive shift in efficiency:
-   **Parameters:** DeltaWorld (0.3B parameters) has over 35x fewer parameters than Cosmos-12B.
-   **FLOPs:** It requires over 2,000x fewer floating-point operations to generate 20 samples compared to the Cosmos baselines.
-   **Token Count:** DeltaTok achieves a $1,024 \times$ reduction in tokens per frame compared to dense spatial grids.

![Performance comparison on semantic segmentation and depth forecasting tasks.](https://paper-assets.alphaxiv.org/figures-normalized/figures/2604.04913v1/x2.png)
*Caption: DeltaWorld demonstrates competitive or superior performance compared to much larger models like Cosmos-12B across various dense forecasting tasks.*

Despite its smaller size, DeltaWorld consistently outperformed Cosmos on dense forecasting benchmarks for Cityscapes, KITTI, and VSPW. In terms of "Best-of-20" scores (the accuracy of the most accurate sample among 20 generated), DeltaWorld achieved higher mIoU for segmentation and lower RMSE for depth estimation. This suggests that the delta token representation, combined with BoM training, effectively captures the variety of possible real-world dynamics.

An ablation study further validated the design choices. While adding BoM training to a standard model initially improved the "best" prediction but hurt the "average" prediction (due to noisy, unrealistic samples), the introduction of DeltaTok stabilized the model. By focusing on the *change* from the previous frame, the model has a natural prior: if the delta token is zero, the scene remains consistent. This prevents the "drifting" or complete scene collapse often seen in other compressed generative models.

## Significance and Future Directions

DeltaWorld demonstrates that generative world modeling does not inherently require massive parameter counts or expensive pixel-level reconstruction. By exploiting the temporal redundancy of video and operating in a semantically rich feature space, it is possible to create models that are both diverse in their predictions and efficient enough for practical use.

The use of delta tokens represents a significant departure from standard video tokenization. It provides a way to compress the complex dynamics of a 3D world into a simple 1D sequence that a standard transformer can process with minimal overhead. The fact that the predictor accounts for only 0.5% of the total inference FLOPs suggests that the bottleneck has shifted from the "thinking" (prediction) to the "seeing" (VFM encoding) and "decoding."

Future research may look into scaling this approach to even longer sequences and more complex environments. While DeltaWorld currently handles mid-term horizons (~0.6s) effectively, error accumulation in the autoregressive loop remains a challenge for very long-term forecasting. Additionally, integrating more sophisticated distributional modeling into the BoM framework could further improve the realism of the generated samples. Overall, DeltaWorld provides a lightweight and robust template for the next generation of efficient, uncertainty-aware autonomous systems.

keyInsights: Encoding only the temporal difference between VFM feature maps into a single 'delta' token significantly reduces data redundancy and computational load for video dynamics modeling.
Combining a Best-of-Many training objective with VFM feature space operation enables efficient, single-pass generation of diverse and plausible future states.

A compact, purely temporal representation via delta tokens can improve both generative world models (for diverse forecasting) and discriminative models (for efficiency) without compromising performance.
originalProblem: Discriminative world models produce single, deterministic predictions, failing to capture diverse plausible outcomes in inherently uncertain environments.

Existing generative world models are computationally inefficient due to pixel-level reconstruction, multiple sequential forward passes for generation, and spatio-temporal redundancy in video representations.
Standard video tokenizers encode each frame into dense spatial tokens, leading to long, redundant sequences when only small changes occur between consecutive frames.
results: DeltaWorld achieves over 35x fewer parameters and more than 2,000x fewer FLOPs for generating 20 samples compared to leading generative world models like Cosmos-4B and Cosmos-12B.

On dense forecasting benchmarks (segmentation, depth estimation), DeltaWorld's 'best-of-20' predictions consistently surpass Cosmos models and discriminative baselines, demonstrating superior accuracy (e.g., +5.6 mIoU on Cityscapes mid-horizon over DINO-world).

The DeltaTok component reduces video representation from 1024 spatial tokens per frame to a single delta token, enabling a 1,024x token reduction per frame and making the predictor's FLOPs negligible (0.5% of total inference FLOPs).
solution: Operate prediction within the feature space of Vision Foundation Models (VFMs) to focus on semantically relevant information rather than computationally intensive pixel-level details.

Implement a 'Best-of-Many' (BoM) training objective, allowing the model to generate multiple distinct future hypotheses in a single forward pass by mapping different stochastic inputs to different plausible outcomes.

Introduce 'DeltaTok,' a novel tokenizer that compresses the *difference* between consecutive VFM feature maps into a single continuous 'delta' token, effectively transforming a 3D spatio-temporal video representation into a 1D temporal sequence.
summary.summary: Researchers from Amazon and Eindhoven University of Technology developed DeltaWorld, an efficient generative world model that employs 'delta tokens' to encode temporal changes and uses a Best-of-Many training objective. The system generates diverse future predictions with over 35 times fewer parameters and 2,000 times fewer FLOPs than prior generative models, while achieving superior predictive accuracy.

title: A Frame is Worth One Token: Efficient Generative World Modeling with Delta Tokens
